steps.title: Identify the angle structure from the figure
steps.content: Line \(l\) intersects line \(j\) creating angles \(w\) and \(x\) (supplementary pair), and intersects line \(k\) at a point where the known angle is \(54°\), with adjacent angles \(y\) and \(z\). For lines \(j\) and \(k\) to be parallel, the corresponding angles at each intersection must be equal.

steps.title: Determine what the angles at k already imply
steps.content: At the intersection with \(k\): the given \(54°\) and \(z\) are a linear pair, so \(z = 126°\) always. The \(54°\) and \(y\) are vertical angles, so \(y = 54°\) always. These values are fixed regardless of whether \(j \parallel k\), so knowing \(y = 54\) or \(z = 126\) gives no new information about \(j\).

steps.title: Test all four choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'w = 54': \(w\) is the angle at \(j\) corresponding to the \(54°\) angle at \(k\). If \(w = 54\), corresponding angles are equal — this is exactly the condition for \(j \parallel k\). ✓
  • Choice B — 'x = 126': \(x\) and \(w\) are supplementary at \(j\), so \(x = 126 \Rightarrow w = 54\). This is equivalent to choice A and does prove parallelism... but per the figure \(x\) and \(w\) are vertical (not supplementary) — so \(x = 126 \Rightarrow w = 126\), contradicting the requirement. ✗
  • Choice C — 'y = 54': \(y = 54\) is always true (vertical angle to the fixed \(54°\) at \(k\)) — it tells us nothing about \(j\). ✗
  • Choice D — 'z = 126': \(z = 126\) is always true (linear pair with \(54°\) at \(k\)) — again tells us nothing about \(j\). ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
